title: Free workshop invite for paid subscribers
questions: How can leaders communicate intent, progress, investment, and ROI more effectively?
What makes a workshop format feel fulfilling and broadly useful beyond a single product?
How should you structure and invite participants for early-stage workshop iteration?
summary: The post invites paid subscribers to a small, free workshop being developed for leaders who want to communicate intent, progress, investment, and ROI more intentionally. It draws on the idea of “north star” workshops that are tangential to a product so participants can learn without needing the product itself. The invitation emphasizes focused, non-broadcast participation to help refine the workshop format and content.

title: New and Past Playbooks (Links)
questions: How do I map how work really happens to design a better operating system?
What are the foundations for choosing and using success metrics and measurement?
How should bets, success metrics, and roadmapping fit together?
What playbooks and resources can help me scope and shape initiatives for success?
summary: The post is a curated index of long-form playbooks and resource libraries aimed at helping new readers quickly find practical guidance. It highlights an OS Discovery playbook and connects it to measurement/success metrics, north star thinking, and roadmapping. The intended audience is teams or practitioners looking for structured frameworks and reusable templates to improve how work is organized and planned.

title: TBM 368: Goals Take Practice
questions: Why do teams struggle with OKRs even when they adopt a shared framework?
What goal-setting challenges do different frameworks (SMART, BHAG, FAST, WOOP, etc.) each address?
How can teams build practice for goal setting more frequently than quarterly OKRs?
What other goal types should teams consider beyond OKRs (inputs, milestones, delivery, launch, north star)?
search_terms: why OKRs don't magically solve goal setting
goal-setting frameworks SMART BHAG FAST WOOP HARD CLEAR GROW PACT
practice goal setting more frequently than quarterly
summary: The post argues that while OKRs improve clarity and review cadence, they cannot eliminate the inherent difficulty of setting and refining goals. It’s aimed at product and team leaders who are seeing OKR struggles and need a mindset shift: goal setting is a continuous practice across many interconnected goal types, not a quarterly exercise.

title: TBM 361: Context, Collaboration, Intent, and Investment
questions: Where should we invest more time in establishing foundational models that stay useful over time?
Does our company have a shared language and approach for funding and investment?
Do we feel like we’re chasing our tails each quarter, and what remains stable across cycles?
How well does strategic intent “stick” in our organization, and is it the right level of specificity?
search_terms: capability trees customer journey KPI trees
summary: The post proposes a practical operating-system framework—context, intent, collaboration, and investment—to help teams distinguish what they know, what they aim for, how people coordinate, and how resources are allocated over time. It’s aimed at leaders and product/strategy practitioners who struggle with oversimplified distinctions like strategy vs execution and want to reduce “hamster wheel” quarterly churn by building durable, shared models.

title: TBM 349: A "Product Transformation" North Star
questions: What percentage of product teams in my company can operate fairly independently with clear line of impact to customers and the business?
How can we tell whether teams are making real progress with actionable leading indicators rather than vague milestones?
What organizational structures prevent teams from moving toward a vision of how work should look?
How should we measure product transformation without causing blame games or defensive reactions?
search_terms: leading indicators for product team success
north star metric naming and blame avoidance
think big work small strategy execution
funding model for 0–1 vs growth margins
product team operating model health metric
summary: The post proposes a “product transformation” north star defined as the percentage of product teams that can operate independently with clear customer impact and demonstrate progress via actionable leading indicators. It argues that many companies get stuck around 50–60% due to structural constraints and legacy inertia, leading to “institutionalized incoherence,” and that measurement must be designed to avoid blame and defensiveness. It’s aimed at product leaders and transformation practitioners who want a practical way to assess and improve how teams work as they scale.

title: TBM 324: Goal-O-Rama
questions: What types of goals should we use in an OKR system beyond just targets?
How do we write anti-goals (NOKRs) that clarify strategy and expectations?
What makes a milestone meaningful versus just an arbitrary date?
How can we connect leading inputs to lagging outcomes while staying in learning mode?
summary: The post presents a practical taxonomy of goal types (e.g., primary/objective, target, anti-goal, guardrail, leading/lagging, milestones, process, adaptive, decision-based, capability-building) and argues for mixing them to improve clarity and execution. It’s aimed at teams and practitioners using OKRs or similar goal systems who want better goal design, stronger learning loops, and fewer misleading milestones.

title: Scoping and Shaping Mini-Book
questions: How do we identify the drivers and constraints that shape our roadmap and execution?
What is the difference between limiting constraints and enabling constraints in planning?
How can float/slack be used to improve delivery and decision-making?
What metrics should we use to define success and align teams around a north star?
summary: The post introduces a skimmable mini-book/playbook focused on scoping and shaping work for success, aimed at people planning products, programs, or team execution. It emphasizes using thought experiments and actionable activities to reason about drivers, limiting and enabling constraints, and float/slack, with connections to success metrics and north star alignment. It’s designed for readers who want flexible, workshop-like guidance they can apply in their own teams.

title: TBM 305: Stop the (Goal) Cascade Madness
questions: Why do goal cascades and multi-layer strategy cascades become optics-driven box-checking?
How should front-line teams structure stable core metrics versus temporary bet metrics?
What signals indicate a team is “works on whatever” and lacks stable domain alignment?
How can leadership shift strategy without forcing teams to reinvent metrics every quarter?
search_terms: stable core metrics and bet metrics
summary: This post argues that goal cascades are frequently ineffective because they confuse hierarchical org charts with causal metric models. It provides a metrics-first approach for front-line teams: maintain stable core metrics over long horizons while using temporary bet metrics to test local initiatives and connect them to core outcomes. The guidance is aimed at leaders and product/engineering organizations trying to improve quarterly goal-setting and strategy execution without building artificial multi-level cascades.

title: TBM 227: Explicit vs. Implicit Strategy
questions: How do you translate strategy into tangible commitments people can be held accountable to?
Why do organizations end up with implicit strategy that only shows up in OKRs and roadmaps?
How do you choose the most critical addressable challenges when prioritization is sensitive?
What practices help teams converge on a shared understanding of strategy?
search_terms: strategy translation to OKRs budgets forecasts
Rumelt crux coherent strategy challenges policies actions
why strategy doesn't become real until OKRs
safe space for hard strategy discussions
summary: The post contrasts explicit, coherent strategy with the implicit strategy that emerges when leaders avoid hard prioritization and instead communicate through goals, budgets, and commitments. It argues that most organizational members experience strategy as real only when it is deployed into day-to-day practices like OKRs, roadmaps, incentives, and constraints. It is aimed at leaders and product leaders trying to improve strategy convergence and alignment by creating time and psychological safety for difficult discussions.

title: TBM 225: Milestones and Stepping Stones
questions: How can we use milestones to create real integration and learning rather than hollow checklists?
What makes a milestone a forcing function that teams can’t ignore?
How do we design milestones that feel appropriately uncomfortable and end-to-end?
Should we think of milestones as linear mile markers or as stepping stones with options?
search_terms: scope cut not work harder milestone
summary: The post argues that the language of “milestones” can be used to improve how teams execute product work by creating forcing-function goals that drive integration, urgency, and learning. It’s aimed at product and delivery leaders who feel stuck between delivery-centric checklists and distant outcome-centric planning, and want a practical way to design near-term milestones (or “stepping stones”) that preserve options while moving “from here to there.”

title: TBM 216: Good Goals/Bad Goals
questions: How can I tell whether a goal is good or bad?
What makes a goal enabling versus constraining in harmful ways?
How do we prevent zombie goals and keep goals updated with new information?
Should we prioritize meaningful impact over perfect measurement when setting goals?
summary: The post argues that effective goal setting goes beyond SMART/OKRs by treating good goals as enabling constraints with guardrails, context, adaptability, and value alignment. It is aimed at individuals and teams who want to improve decision-making and execution by identifying and avoiding “bad goals” that create rigidity, distraction, performative behavior, or even exploitation. It also encourages readers to evaluate what behaviors goals actually drive and to iterate goal-setting practices over time.

title: TBM 213: Goal Cascades vs. High-Conviction Models
questions: How can we tell whether our OKR goal cascade is creating high conviction or just wishful thinking?
What’s the difference between transactional OKRs and long-term OKRs that require a high-conviction model?
How do we build a high-conviction model that links actionable inputs to sustainable outcomes?
What is the “irrational-looking” but high-conviction practice at our company that outsiders might not understand?
search_terms: goal cascades vs high conviction models
how to build conviction in planning
summary: The post argues that many OKR goal cascades fail because they generate low conviction when they link non-actionable goals to uncertain long-term outcomes. It’s aimed at product and leadership teams trying to improve planning, alignment, and decision quality by using high-conviction models (often supported by a north star metric) to connect actionable inputs to sustainable growth.

title: TBM 3/52: Real-World Learning Review
questions: How should we brutally critique our view of the world from six weeks ago?
Why might our North Star Metric not reflect customer value, and what should we change?
How do we translate a strategy map into measurable drivers, assumptions, and leverage points?
What does it mean to set goals when the metrics are hard to measure and we’re still learning?
summary: The post documents a founder-led learning review session for an early-stage SaaS startup, focused on how to update strategy using driver maps, metrics, and explicit assumptions. It’s aimed at founders and product leaders who want a practical way to critique their current model of the world, identify leverage points, and set exploratory goals when measurement is difficult.

title: TBM 55/52: Making a Model (Step-by-Step)
questions: How do you go about making your models and frameworks step by step?
What inputs and outputs should you map when building a community health model?
How do you incorporate prior work without copying it blindly?
How can you turn messy sticky-note themes into a flywheel model that supports prioritization?
summary: This post is a practical, step-by-step walkthrough of how to build a model from scratch, using live diagramming and iterative refinement in a tool like Miro. It’s aimed at people (especially in product/growth and community work) who want a repeatable method for turning messy ideas into a structured framework that can support prioritization and discussion.

title: TBM 42/52: Outcome Super Prompt
questions: How do we help teams focus on what they’re measuring rather than how they’ll measure it?
What does a good driver statement look like in a fill-in-the-blank template?
How can we specify outcomes precisely using constrained choices for variable definitions?
How do we connect initiatives to drivers and outcomes through a traceable structure?
search_terms: A B C D metrics prompt
Miro workshop activity drivers constraints floats
summary: The post presents a facilitation activity (“outcome super prompt”) for helping teams write specific driver/outcome statements using a structured A-B-C-D template. It’s aimed at product and growth teams who want to clarify what they’re measuring (and for whom) before deciding how to measure it, with an optional advanced step to trace from initiatives up to root drivers.

title: TBM 42/52: Fluffy & Ambiguous
questions: How do I write objectives that leave room for creativity without becoming fluffy and ambiguous?
Why do my PMs seem to agree but produce roadmaps that feel scattered?
What should I do when newer PMs lack the implicit context that made earlier alignment easy?
How can I tell whether an objective is specific enough to inspire coherent actions and good questions?
search_terms: onboarding PMs with context and clarity
summary: This post helps product leaders and PM orgs craft objectives and guiding principles that preserve creativity while avoiding ambiguity that causes misaligned roadmaps. It explains why newer PMs may respond with ambivalence when they lack the implicit context that more tenured PMs rely on, and offers practical tests for whether objectives are specific, opinionated, and question-inviting. Intended for product leaders, product managers, and teams using OKR-style planning who want clearer strategy without over-prescription.

title: TBM 41/51: Why Goal Cascades are Harmful (and What to Do Instead)
questions: Why do goal cascades lead to misalignment and reduced congruence across teams?
How can we set goals without tying them to the org chart hierarchy?
What should goals be based on: hierarchical priorities or driver models and leverage points?
How do we distinguish inputs, outputs, and measurement when designing a goal system?
search_terms: network of goals instead of hierarchy
summary: The post argues that hierarchical goal cascades (few top-level goals replicated fractally) often create misalignment by reinforcing org boundaries, oversimplifying variable relationships, and conflating inputs/outputs with structure. It recommends an alternative for strategy and planning: build an org-agnostic driver model with measurement, identify leverage points, and then set goals based on the model (potentially with multiple layers and time horizons). The guidance is aimed at leaders and teams designing goal-setting and operating mechanisms for organizational congruence.

title: North Star Framework Template & Activity Library
questions: Where can I find reusable North Star Framework templates and workshop activities?
What materials come with a North Star workshop template library (videos, articles, playbook links)?
How do I access and use the North Star templates in Miro?
search_terms: north star metric workshop miro board
summary: This post shares a reusable Miro-based template and activity library for running North Star Framework workshops. It’s aimed at product teams and facilitators who want ready-to-use workshop assets, with guidance materials like videos and curated references. Readers can copy the templates (with a free Miro account) and use the board as a collaborative resource.

title: Free training sessions (to help me become a better trainer)
questions: How can I become a better trainer by changing my facilitation style?
What learning outcomes should I use to design short training sessions?
How do we communicate and deploy strategy so teams stay aligned?
How do we choose metrics and measure the impact of our work?
search_terms: training from the back of the room
how to be a better trainer
summary: The post offers 30 short, guided training sessions (55 minutes) for leaders in product, design, engineering, and analytics who want to improve how they teach and how they execute strategy. It is inspired by the idea of “training from the back of the room,” emphasizing learners doing most of the talking while the trainer guides toward specific learning outcomes. The sessions cover strategy development, communication, measurement, prioritization, alignment, and team structuring.

title: TBM 31/52: Bet Up Activity
questions: How can we make our implicit strategy explicit using current work-in-progress?
What workshop activity can help teams reverse engineer a driver tree from real efforts?
Do teams’ current initiatives connect to meaningful outcomes we can measure?
Where are we missing measurement focus or actionable levers?
search_terms: strategy flip from work in progress
North Star strategy down vs bet up
summary: This post presents a workshop facilitation activity (“Bet Up”) for product and strategy teams to reverse engineer a driver tree from current work-in-progress. It is designed for groups that want to surface implicit strategy, align efforts to meaningful outcomes, and scan for measurement focus and missing levers. The author emphasizes that the method scales to many teams and tends to converge on shared drivers.

title: TBM 6/52: Inputs First. Bets Next
questions: How do we stop quarterly whiplash when we attach OKRs and metrics after choosing work?
What does an inputs-first approach look like for setting goals and success metrics?
How can a persistent driver model help stabilize planning across quarters?
What are minimally viable measurements and leading indicators for each layer of a driver tree?
summary: This post argues that teams should set vision and a stable 12–18 month focus first, then derive metrics and OKRs from a persistent model rather than rushing to attach measurements to a list of features. It’s aimed at product and delivery leaders who want to reduce end-of-quarter chaos and move from “ship and forget” toward progress on core missions using driver trees and minimally viable measurements.

title: TBM 45/52: Big Disrupted Enterprises vs.  "The Best"
questions: Why is organizational transformation so much harder in disrupted enterprises than in digital-product-first companies?
Which practices from Silicon Valley (OKRs, empowered teams) actually transfer to large enterprises, and which do not?
Are digital-product-first “best” companies healthy and psychologically safe places to work, or is the pedestal partly luck and money?
How should leaders design change efforts when investor patience is limited and inertia is strong?
search_terms: north star inputs and flywheels deep dive
organizational inertia and status quo bias
business agility antipatterns sooner safer happier
summary: The post contrasts rapidly growing digital-product-first companies with large disrupted enterprises, arguing that transformation difficulty and constraints differ dramatically. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners designing change, especially those tempted to copy BigTech playbooks like OKRs and empowered teams. The author calls for more empathy and contextual awareness when evaluating “the best” and “lumbering” organizations.

title: TBM 35/52: Vanity Metric vs. Meaningful Metric Activity
questions: How do we distinguish vanity metrics from meaningful metrics for our North Star?
What North Star Metric name would feel inspiring and human-centric to our team?
How can we avoid analysis paralysis when defining measurement for a North Star Metric?
What is the fastest way to align a team on OKRs and a North Star Framework?
search_terms: north star metric vanity vs meaningful
how to name a north star metric
summary: The post offers a practical facilitation shortcut for teams defining a North Star Metric within an OKRs/North Star Framework context. It contrasts vanity metrics with meaningful, strategy-validating metrics and uses a human-centric visualization exercise to help teams overcome measurement analysis paralysis. It’s aimed at product and analytics leaders who need a fast way to align on a compelling metric and start measuring.

title: TBM 28/53: Words Before Numbers (North Star Statement Exercise)
questions: How do we clarify our own strategy and assumptions before defining metrics?
What prompts or steps help teams refine a North Star statement quickly?
Should we define measurement before or after we align on a strategy narrative?
How can we choose metrics that support learning and reduce uncertainty?
summary: The post shares a workshop exercise for product teams using the North Star Framework that emphasizes clarifying words, assumptions, and strategy before selecting metrics. It’s aimed at teams that tend to jump straight to measurement or copy other companies, and it guides them to refine a short North Star statement and only then consider metrics that enable learning and reduce uncertainty.

title: TBM 24/52: Beware of the Cascade
questions: How can I tell when our goals and work are turning into a damaging cascade?
What makes the North Star Framework different from a traditional goal cascade?
How do I redesign goal/metric relationships to improve flexibility and customer relevance?
What practical steps can we take to flatten layers and reduce input-focused, time-bound work?
search_terms: goal decomposition vs north star framework
input focused vs output focused goals
summary: This post explains how hierarchical goal/work cascades can degrade flexibility, creativity, and customer relevance by pushing teams toward prescriptive, input-focused, time-bound execution. It contrasts cascading structures with the North Star Framework, arguing that metrics can guide work without a top-down strategy-to-project hierarchy. It offers practical ways to recognize and fix cascade dynamics, including flattening levels and reframing relationships as leading indicators.

title: TBM 16/52: Goal Clarity (and Embracing Different Perspectives)
questions: Why do teams stay misaligned even when everyone agrees on the same goal statement?
How can we make goals clearer without over-specifying solutions?
What conversations should we have to surface assumptions and out-of-scope boundaries in a goal?
How do we handle different experience levels when interpreting and acting on goals?
search_terms: interpretation gaps in OKRs or quarterly goals
why goals fail to align teams
summary: The post argues that even a well-written quarterly goal will be interpreted differently by team members, leading to misalignment. It is aimed at managers and team leads who want practical ways to improve goal clarity through shared understanding, explicit conversations about interpretations and decision impact, and psychological safety to iterate on goals. It also emphasizes respecting different experience levels so that newer or less confident contributors can engage meaningfully.

title: TBM 25/53: Persistent Models vs. Point-In-Time Goals
questions: How do you balance persistent models (strategy-level) with point-in-time goals (quarterly work) in your organization?
Why do quarterly OKRs feel like a “big deal” for some teams, and how does the absence of persistent models contribute?
What should teams build first: a persistent model or initiative/OKR goal setting?
How can teams use a north star metric and causal inputs to inform OKRs without implying a specific end date?
summary: The post contrasts persistent models (e.g., north star metrics and causal inputs that endure for 1–3 years) with point-in-time goals (e.g., quarterly OKRs tied to a specific time span). It argues that teams struggle and overcomplicate quarterly goal setting when they lack persistent models, and that coaching should emphasize building persistent models before setting initiative goals. It is aimed at product and strategy leaders who want better alignment between long-term value creation and short-term execution.

title: TBM 6/53: Way and Why
questions: How do I introduce a framework like the North Star Framework in my organization without becoming “the framework person”?
Why do people misread my intent when I push a process or method?
What does it mean to lead with the why instead of the way in team communication?
How can I test whether my teammates share the underlying problem or opportunity before proposing a solution?
search_terms: leading with the why vs the way
how to introduce OKRs or north star framework
avoid being pigeonholed as the process person
north star framework autonomy with alignment
how to sell a framework to a team
experiment with distancing from the solution
summary: The post offers guidance for leaders and practitioners on how to introduce popular frameworks (using the North Star Framework as an example) without being perceived as pushing process for its own sake. It argues that adoption succeeds more often when you lead with the underlying why—an opportunity, problem, or observation—using specific, first-person language to reduce intent/perception gaps. It’s aimed at people trying to influence teams toward better ways of working while maintaining trust and shared ownership.

title: TBM 2/53: Three Options
questions: What options did we consider but reject, and why?
How can we frame the problem so the team can generate at least three distinct, credible paths to the goal?
Does our OKR objective unintentionally eliminate alternative options (e.g., “deliver X” with no other viable approach)?
When should we revisit the selected strategy, and what evidence would change our confidence?
summary: This post argues that product and strategy conversations move faster and build more trust when teams explicitly surface three options they considered, including the ones they rejected and the reasoning behind that choice. It’s aimed at product managers, designers, and coaches who want better decision-making hygiene, clearer context for new team members, and stronger goal-setting (including OKR quality) through forcing alternative paths to be considered.
